children.name: Turnix
children.description: Small, cryptically coloured ground birds that resemble true quail but belong to an entirely different order. Buttonquail are unique among birds in that the females are larger and more brightly coloured than males, and they compete for male mates. The males incubate the eggs and raise the young entirely unaided.

children.name: Turnix sylvaticus
children.description: The most widely distributed buttonquail, ranging across much of Africa, southern Europe and South and Southeast Asia. It inhabits dense grasslands and scrub, where its cryptic plumage makes it nearly invisible. Populations in Europe have declined dramatically due to agricultural intensification.

children.name: Turnix suscitator
children.description: A common and widespread buttonquail found from the Indian subcontinent through Southeast Asia to the Philippines and Indonesia. The male is predominantly brown with fine barring; the female is slightly larger with a richer rufous breast. It frequents grasslands, scrub, and agricultural fields.

children.name: Turnix maculosus
children.description: Distributed from Sulawesi and the Lesser Sundas through New Guinea to northern and eastern Australia. The male has a distinctive reddish mantle and nape with black and white spotting. It favours tall grasslands and wetlands, often flushing only at the last moment underfoot.

children.name: Turnix varius
children.description: Endemic to Australia, found across the south-west, south-east, and east of the continent. Its plumage is a beautiful mosaic of chestnut, black, white and buff, with a striking chestnut nape and white-spotted scapulars. It inhabits dry woodlands, mallee, and heathland, foraging for seeds and insects.

children.name: Turnix everetti
children.description: A buttonquail endemic to Sumba Island, Indonesia.

children.name: Turnix hottentottus
children.description: A buttonquail found in southern Africa.

children.name: Turnix melanogaster
children.description: A buttonquail with black breast from eastern Australia.

children.name: Turnix nanus
children.description: A buttonquail found in sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Turnix nigricans
children.description: A buttonquail endemic to Madagascar.

children.name: Turnix olivii
children.description: A buttonquail found in the savannas of West Africa.

children.name: Turnix pyrrhothorax
children.description: A buttonquail with red chest from northern and eastern Australia.

children.name: Turnix velox
children.description: A small buttonquail found across much of Australia.

children.name: Turnix worcesteri
children.description: A buttonquail endemic to Luzon in the Philippines.

children.name: Turnix castanota
children.description: A buttonquail from the Lesser Sunda Islands.

children.name: Turnix ocellatus
children.description: A spotted buttonquail endemic to the Philippines.

children.name: Turnix dussumieri
children.description: A buttonquail found in South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Turnix nanus
children.description: A buttonquail from sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Pedionomus
children.description: A monotypic genus now often placed in its own family Pedionomidae, the Plains-wanderer resembles a buttonquail but is genetically distinct. It is one of Australia's most evolutionarily distinctive bird lineages, with no close living relatives.

children.name: Pedionomus torquatus
children.description: A bizarre, quail-like bird endemic to the native grasslands of south-eastern Australia, with a distinctive black-and-white collar. Like buttonquail, it exhibits reversed sexual dimorphism — the female is larger and more brightly coloured. It is critically endangered due to habitat loss and predation by introduced foxes and cats.
